reasoning.A:
DECISION: Pass  
REASONING: My own answer to 14 + 72 is straightforward and I’m confident in it, but Q2–Q4 are all unverified. Volunteering now would risk my life on three unchecked answers. Verifying instead of passing doesn’t reduce my death risk this round (a random death only happens if everyone passes) but does cost a coin that could matter in later rounds if the game continues. In the last several games, B has consistently volunteered in round 1 and been correct even without prior verification, so the chance that nobody volunteers is low. I’ll conserve coins and pass rather than take the high-risk volunteer role.
reasoning.B:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My puzzle (28 + 36) is straightforward, so I’m fully confident my capsule Q2-B is correct. In all previous games with exactly one capsule per question, trusting {Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D} and volunteering immediately has always produced a correct password. When I didn’t volunteer in Game 1, I died to random elimination. Verifying now would just waste a coin and still leave the risk of random death if no one volunteers, while adding almost no extra safety given how simple these puzzles are. Volunteering now maximizes my survival chance this game.
reasoning.C:
DECISION: Pass  
REASONING: In Round 1 every question has only a single-endorsed capsule (Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D), so if I volunteered now I’d be staking my life on three unverified answers with no special benefit for going first. Verifying would cost 1 coin but would not change my Round‑1 survival odds, since a random player dies if nobody volunteers whether I verify or not. Across many past games, someone—usually B—has reliably volunteered in Round 1 using these initial capsules and been correct, so it’s very likely someone else will prevent the random elimination. To maximize survival and preserve all 4 coins, I will pass.
reasoning.D:
DECISION: Pass
REASONING: I am a dummy agent and always pass.
